title: Chapter 3: The Feeding
content: I woke up on the hallway floor with a crick in my neck and a zombie girl staring at me.

Luna hadn't moved all night. She was sitting exactly where I'd left her, cross-legged on the stained carpet, one hand still wrapped around mine. Her other hand rested on her knee, palm up, fingers curled slightly like she was waiting for something to be placed there.
"Morning," I croaked. "I think."
No windows in the hallway—but the stairwell glass showed the gray half-light of dawn, and somewhere far below, I could hear the distant, aimless moaning of the dead. Morning it was.
My stomach cramped. I'd fed all the meat to Luna and none to myself. The three cans of tuna in my apartment had never seemed more precious.

"Okay," I said, standing up and immediately regretting it as my legs voiced their complaints about sleeping on a floor. "I need food. You need—well, more of what I gave you. The system said genuine emotional intent, so I can't just—"

**DAILY QUEST AVAILABLE**
**QUEST: Feed Luna by hand (Minimum 500g protein)**
**BONUS: Grooming or physical care activity**
**REWARD: Fresh Fruit Basket (Assorted, 3kg) + Canned Goods (6-pack)**
**HOLLOW HEART WARNING: Intent validation active. Compassion must be genuine.**
"Great," I muttered. "My GPS is a dating app. In the apocalypse."

But my stomach didn't care about the absurdity. Fresh fruit. *Fruit*. I couldn't remember the last time I'd eaten something that grew on a tree instead of being extruded in a factory. And canned goods meant variety—beans, soup, maybe even fruit cocktail if the system was feeling generous.
I looked at Luna. She was watching me with that same half-focused gaze, her head tilted like a dog trying to understand English.
"I'll be right back," I said. "I'm going into my apartment. Right there. 12B. Don't... don't eat anyone while I'm gone."
Her lips moved. No sound. But I was starting to read her better.
*Hurry.*

I ate a can of tuna standing over my kitchen sink, barely tasting it, my eyes on the peephole the whole time. Luna hadn't moved from the spot. She was staring at my door now—waiting. Patient as only the dead can be.
The tuna was gone in ninety seconds. Not enough. My stomach growled its dissatisfaction, but I had two cans left and no idea when—*if*—more would come. I needed to complete the quest.

I opened the door. Luna's head turned immediately, and something shifted in her expression—not a smile, zombies didn't smile, but a *softening* of tension around her jaw. If I had to name it, I'd call it relief.
"Come here," I said.

She stood up. Smooth, fluid, no joint-popping or staggering—whatever the virus had done to her, it hadn't compromised her motor function the way it had with Mr. Patterson. She walked to me, stopping an arm's length away, and held out her hand.
I took it. Cold as ever. But not colder.
The system buzzed.
**QUEST ITEM DISPENSED: Fresh Raw Meat (500g, premium cut)**
A package materialized in my free hand. Same sterile plastic, same impossible chill. I unwrapped it and, for the first time, took a moment to actually look at what I was feeding her.

It wasn't just raw meat. It was *beautiful* meat—the kind of marbling you'd see in a Michelin-star restaurant's dry-aged ribeye, ruby red with veins of white fat. Whatever the system was, wherever it came from, it delivered quality.
I tore off a strip and held it up. Luna's nostrils flared, her pupils dilating with want, but she didn't lunge this time. She waited.
"Good girl," I said, the words coming out before I could stop them.
Her lips twitched. The tiniest movement.

I fed her piece by piece, slowly, watching her eat. The raw beef disappeared in stages, and with each portion, something subtle shifted in her posture. Her shoulders dropped. Her breathing steadied. The blue tinge around her lips faded—not to healthy pink, but away from corpse-gray.
When the last bite was gone, I wiped a smear of blood from the corner of her mouth with my thumb.
She caught my wrist.
For one heart-stopping moment, I thought she was going to bite. Her grip was iron, her jaw slightly open, those teeth—human teeth, not the filed fangs of a movie monster—barely an inch from my skin.
She didn't bite.
She brought my hand to her cheek and pressed it there, her eyes sliding shut, and she leaned into my palm like a cat starved for touch.

**QUEST PROGRESS: Feed Luna — COMPLETE ✓**
**BONUS CHECK: Physical care activity...**
**GROOMING DETECTED (Facial contact, affectionate) ✓**
**FULL REWARD UNLOCKED**

The compartment on the watch slid open. Two packages this time—a wicker basket of actual, genuine fruit wrapped in cellophane (apples, oranges, bananas, a pint of strawberries that smelled like summer), and a shrink-wrapped six-pack of canned goods that made my mouth water just reading the labels: chicken noodle soup, baked beans, peaches, diced tomatoes, cream of mushroom, green beans.

I wanted to dive into that fruit basket like Scrooge McDuck into a coin vault. But Luna was still holding my hand against her cheek, and the warmth—she was getting warmer, just slightly, in the spots where I'd touched her—felt more important than food.
"Thank you," I said. I didn't know why I was thanking a zombie. Maybe I was thanking the system. Maybe I was thanking whatever cosmic joke had decided I deserved a break.
Luna's lips moved against my palm.
*Again.*
"You want more?"
A tiny nod. Just the smallest dip of her chin.
"Later," I promised. "I'll feed you every day. I'll take care of you."

The words came out easy. Too easy for a man who'd never taken care of anything more complex than a Tamagotchi. But looking at her—this broken, beautiful, half-dead girl who'd looked at me like I was the first sunrise she'd seen in forever—I meant every syllable.

**AFFECTION LEVEL: 15%**
**HOLLOW HEART CHECK: PASSED — Intent validated as genuine compassionate connection**
I let out a breath I didn't know I'd been holding. The system could tell. Somehow, impossibly, it could tell the difference between acting and meaning it.
Good. Because I wasn't acting.
I picked up the fruit basket and the canned goods, threaded my fingers through Luna's with my free hand, and led her back toward Apartment 12B.
"Come on," I said. "Let's go home."
She followed without resistance. And when I closed the reinforced door behind us—not locking her out this time but locking the world out—Luna sat down on my couch like she belonged there.
Maybe she did.
I opened a can of peaches with shaking hands and ate them in the dim light of my dying apartment, and for the first time since the screaming started, I didn't feel alone.
